languages.title: Get to Learn Some of Our Languages
languages.description: Africa is home to thousands of languages, each carrying history, identity, and culture. Explore common greetings and words from different African communities and connect with the continent through language.

swahili.name: Swahili
swahili.tooltip: A common greeting is 'Habari?', meaning 'How are you?'

kikuyu.name: Kikuyu
kikuyu.tooltip: A common greeting is 'Wî mwega?', meaning 'How are you?'

luo.name: Luo
luo.tooltip: A common greeting is 'Nadi?', meaning 'How are you?'

kalenjin.name: Kalenjin
kalenjin.tooltip: A common greeting is 'Chamgei?', meaning 'How are you?'

kamba.name: Kamba
kamba.tooltip: A common greeting is 'Uvoo?', meaning 'How are you?'

somali.name: Somali
somali.tooltip: A common greeting is 'Iska warran?', meaning 'How are you?'

turkana.name: Turkana
turkana.tooltip: A common greeting is 'Ejoka?', meaning 'How are you/Hello.'

tribes.title: Explore Our Tribes
tribes.subtitle: Journey through Africa's diverse tribes and timeless traditions

oromo.name: Oromo
oromo.description: Rooted in Ethiopia, the Oromo people carry a proud legacy of tradition, unity, and cultural identity. They are the country's largest ethnic group, making up about one-third of the population with around 40 million people.

zulu.name: Zulu
zulu.description: From South Africa, the Zulu people carry a powerful legacy of tradition, resilience, and identity, representing over 11 million people.

igbo.name: Igbo
igbo.description: Rooted in Nigeria, the Igbo people are one of the country's largest ethnic groups, numbering over 30 million, known for their rich traditions, entrepreneurship, and strong communal values.

socialImpact.title: Our Social Impact
socialImpact.description: We believe in giving back to the communities that inspire us. Through our platform, we support local artisans, preserve cultural heritage, and create economic opportunities across Africa.

artisans.title: Supporting Local Artisans
artisans.description: We connect local craftspeople directly with global markets, ensuring fair compensation for their work.

heritage.title: Preserving Heritage
heritage.description: Our digital archive helps preserve endangered languages, traditions, and cultural practices for future generations.

partners.title: Our Partners
partners.description: We collaborate with organizations that share our vision of preserving and celebrating African culture. Together, we're making a bigger impact.

faq.title: Frequently Asked
faq.subtitle2: Get answers to common questions about Africa

whatIs.question: What is Magical Africa?
whatIs.answer: Magical Africa is a platform dedicated to preserving, celebrating, and sharing the rich cultural heritage of Africa with the world. We connect people with authentic African cultures, languages, traditions, and communities.

contribute.question: How can I contribute to Magical Africa?
contribute.answer: You can contribute by sharing stories, photos, or knowledge about your community. Simply sign up and use our contribution tools to add content about your tribe's traditions, language, and heritage.

authentic.question: Is the content on Magical Africa authentic?
authentic.answer: Yes! All our content is sourced directly from community members and cultural experts. We work closely with local communities to ensure accuracy and authenticity in everything we share.

learnLanguage.question: How do I learn an African language on this platform?
learnLanguage.answer: We offer language learning resources including common phrases, audio pronunciations from native speakers, and cultural context to help you understand and appreciate African languages.

purchase.question: Can I purchase authentic African products?
purchase.answer: Yes! Our marketplace features authentic cultural merchandise handcrafted by African artisans. Every purchase supports local communities and helps preserve traditional craftsmanship.

contact.description: Have questions about African culture, heritage, or our collections? Reach out to us and discover the stories, traditions, and creativity that make Africa unique. We'd love to connect with you and share the true magic of Africa.

footer.tagline: Where the Real African Story lives

auth.joinTextEnd: and discover Africa's culture, heritage, stories, and traditions.

maasai.name: Maasai
maasai.desc: Semi-nomadic warriors known for distinctive customs and dress.

ashanti.name: Ashanti
ashanti.desc: Known for their gold craftsmanship and the powerful Ashanti Empire.

hausa.name: Hausa
hausa.desc: One of the largest ethnic groups in Africa, known for trade and commerce.

zulu.name: Zulu
zulu.desc: Known for their warrior culture, beadwork, and the famous Zulu Kingdom.

yoruba.name: Yoruba
yoruba.desc: Rich in mythology, art, and ancient city-states like Ife and Oyo.

kikuyu.name: Kikuyu
kikuyu.desc: The largest ethnic group in Kenya, known for agriculture and trade.

igbo.name: Igbo
igbo.desc: Known for their entrepreneurial spirit and rich artistic traditions across generations.

amhara.name: Amhara
amhara.desc: Historically dominant group in Ethiopia with ancient Christian heritage.

berber.name: Berber
berber.desc: Indigenous people of North Africa with ancient traditions and languages.

swahili.name: Swahili
swahili.desc: Coastal people known for their maritime trade and cultural fusion for centuries.

wolof.name: Wolof
wolof.desc: Dominant ethnic group in Senegal known for their hospitality and unity.

fulani.name: Fulani
fulani.desc: Nomadic pastoralists spread across West Africa with distinct traditions.

history.title: History & Migration
history.p1: The Maasai are a Nilotic ethnic group inhabiting northern, central and southern Kenya and northern Tanzania. They are among the best known local populations internationally due to their residence near many game parks of the African Great Lakes and their distinctive customs and dress.
history.p2: The Maasai speak the Maa language, a member of the Nilotic language family related to Dinka and Nuer. They are known for their unique culture, customs, and dress, as well as their reputation as fierce warriors and cattle herders. The Maasai have maintained much of their traditional lifestyle despite pressure from modern development.

identity.title: Identity & Lifestyle
identity.description: The Maasai are semi-nomadic pastoralists who have traditionally relied on cattle, sheep, and goats for their livelihood. Cattle hold special significance in Maasai culture, representing wealth, status, and sustenance. The Maasai measure a man's wealth by the number of cattle and children he has.

language.title: The Maa
language.description: The Maa language is the traditional language of the Maasai people of East Africa. It is spoken mainly in Kenya and northern Tanzania, and it belongs to the Nilotic language family. Maa is more than just a means of communication — it plays a central role in preserving Maasai culture, identity, and oral traditions.

proverbs.meishoo: It takes a village to raise a child

audio.title: Listen to Traditional Greetings
audio.description: Learn authentic Maa pronunciation directly from native speakers, preserving the true sounds, rhythm, and cultural meaning of the Maasai language.

clothing.title: Traditional Clothing & Adornment
clothing.description: The Maasai are renowned for their distinctive red clothing called "shuka." Red is the most important color, symbolizing bravery, unity, and blood. Both men and women wear elaborate beaded jewelry, with different colors and patterns signifying age, marital status, and social position.

eunoto.title: Eunoto
eunoto.desc: Coming of age ceremony where warriors transition to junior elders

emuratare.title: Emuratare
emuratare.desc: Circumcision ceremony marking transition to warrior status

nyamaChoma.title: Nyama Choma
nyamaChoma.desc: Roasted meat, especially goat and beef

olpurda.title: Olpurda
olpurda.desc: Soup made from meat, bones, and herbs

laibonLenana.name: Laibon Lenana
laibonLenana.desc: A powerful Maasai spiritual leader who played a key role during British colonization.

olonana.name: Olonana
olonana.desc: Chief and Laibon who negotiated treaties to protect Maasai lands.

mbatian.name: Mbatian
mbatian.desc: The most famous Laibon, known for his prophecies and unifying the Maasai.

oleSaibul.name: Ole Saibul
oleSaibul.desc: A prominent Maasai politician and advocate for pastoralist rights in Kenya.

oleNtimama.name: William Ole Ntimama
oleNtimama.desc: Kenyan politician who championed Maasai land rights for decades.

oleLenku.name: Joseph Ole Lenku
oleLenku.desc: Current Governor of Kajiado County and former Cabinet Secretary.

nature.title: Relationship with Nature
nature.description: The Maasai have lived in harmony with wildlife for centuries, sharing the same ecosystem with lions, elephants, and other wildlife. Their pastoral lifestyle and traditional beliefs have helped preserve vast areas of wilderness.

cattle.name: Cattle
cattle.desc: Sacred gift from Enkai (God), central to Maasai identity and survival

lions.name: Lions
lions.desc: Respected as symbols of bravery; historically hunted by warriors in coming-of-age rituals

elephants.name: Elephants
elephants.desc: Revered for wisdom and strength; protected through traditional conservation

giraffes.name: Giraffes
giraffes.desc: Seen as graceful beings sharing their grazing lands

antiques.title: Traditional Artifacts & Antiques
antiques.description: The Maasai people have crafted distinctive tools, weapons, and adornments for centuries. These artifacts represent their rich cultural heritage and continue to be symbols of identity and tradition.

rungu.name: Rungu (Throwing Club)
rungu.desc: Traditional wooden club used by warriors for protection and hunting

shuka.name: Shuka (Red Cloth)
shuka.desc: Iconic red checkered cloth worn as traditional dress

beadedJewelry.name: Beaded Jewelry
beadedJewelry.desc: Intricate beadwork representing status, age, and identity

spear.name: Spear (Enkudi)
spear.desc: Symbol of manhood and primary weapon of Maasai warriors

shield.name: Shield (Olongu)
shield.desc: Oval shields painted with distinctive patterns representing clan identity

calabash.name: Calabash (Enkukuri)
calabash.desc: Gourd containers used for storing milk and blood mixtures

preserve.title: Help Us Preserve Maasai Heritage
preserve.description: Do you have stories, photos, or knowledge to share about Maasai culture?

hero.title: About Magical Africa
hero.tagline: Preserving African Languages. Empowering communities. Building the future.

background.paragraph1: Magical.africa is a pan-African organization working at the intersection of African languages, culture, education, and technology. We exist to support the continued use, growth, and sustainability of community languages across the continent.
background.paragraph2: African languages are central to education, livelihoods, and social cohesion, yet many remain underrepresented in formal systems and digital spaces. Magical Africa addresses this gap through a community-centered, partnership-driven approach that brings together local knowledge holders, educators, researchers, and technology partners.
background.paragraph3: By using technology as an enabling tool not a replacement we help integrate African languages and cultural knowledge into modern systems in ethical, inclusive, and sustainable ways.

approach.title: Our Approach
approach.description: We combine culture, technology, and education to empower communities and preserve African heritage.

datastewardship.title: African Language & Cultural Data Stewardship
datastewardship.description: We partner with communities to document, curate, and steward African languages and cultural practices in ways that respect local norms, histories, and priorities. Our work includes:

aiDataCollection.title: Community-Centered AI & Data Collection
aiDataCollection.description: Magical Africa designs and leads ethical, community-driven data collection processes that support inclusive AI development for African languages. We focus on:

education.title: Education & Cultural Knowledge Projects
education.description: We work in schools, learning spaces, and cultural settings across different African regions to support language learning, knowledge transmission, and intergenerational exchange. We focus on:

principles.title: Our Principles
items: Pan-African by design - Grounded locally, connected continent-wide
Community First - Communities are collaborators, not data sources
Ethical by Default - Consent, fairness, and privacy are non-negotiable
Cultural Respect - Language and culture are living systems
Open & Collaborative - We support open knowledge and shared learning
Human-Centered AI - Technology must serve people, not extract from them

research.title: AI-Assisted Research & Quality Review
items: Assist with transcription and annotation workflows
Support quality checks always paired with human validation

team.title: Our key Team Members
team.description: Preserving Culture, Language, and Identity Across Generations

gloria.name: Gloria Machoka
gloria.bio: Gloria founded Magical Africa with a vision to preserve African languages, celebrate culture, and empower youth through learning, creativity, and community.

steve.name: steve Kombo
steve.bio: Passionate about guiding the mission to preserve African stories and build digital spaces that connect people, languages, and heritage.

joel.name: Joel Makori
joel.bio: Community Engagement Manager at Magical Africa, connecting people, supporting community initiatives, and ensuring vibrant participation in culture, storytelling, and heritage programs.

Edewait.name: Edewait Eric
Edewait.bio: Leads content development by researching indigenous languages, documenting endangered words and stories, and safeguarding cultural meaning across the Magical Africa community.

lorna.name: Lorna Wanderi
lorna.bio: Designs intuitive and engaging user experiences that make learning African languages and exploring culture simple, accessible, and enjoyable.

ian.name: Ian Cheruiyot
ian.bio: Builds language technology that helps preserve and process African languages, turning linguistic data into tools that support learning, translation, and cultural preservation.

collins.name: Collins Omondi
collins.bio: Builds intelligent systems that power personalized learning, recommendations, and AI-driven features across the Magical Africa platform.

cta.title: Join Us in Preserving The Real African Story
cta.description: Africa doesn't need to be explained by outsiders. We will tell our own story with dignity, accuracy, creativity, and pride.

phrases.title: Phrases
phrases.description: An expression suggesting that curiosity, when unchecked, can lead one into risky or dangerous situations.
phrases.teaching: Some questions or actions can lead to trouble.

music.title: Cultural Music
music.intro: African music is one of the world's oldest traditions, deeply woven into community life through ceremonies, celebrations, storytelling, dance, and spiritual practices.

events.title: Cultural Events
events.intro: African cultural events celebrate heritage by uniting communities through music, dance, art, food, and storytelling, honoring tradition while embracing modern African identity.
events.festivalDescription: A week-long celebration featuring traditional dance performances, drum circles, art exhibitions, and authentic African cuisine from across the continent.

popularCourses.title: Our courses
popularCourses.subtitle: Explore a wide range of courses taught by passionate African educators — from traditional crafts to cultural arts, there is something for everyone.
popularCourses.potteryDesc: Learn the ancient art of hand-building and wheel-throwing pottery. Create bowls, vases, and traditional African vessels using authentic techniques passed down through generations.
popularCourses.instrumentMakingDesc: Craft traditional African instruments from scratch — from the djembe drum to the kora. Understand tonal construction, materials, and the cultural significance of each instrument.
popularCourses.weavingDesc: Master the art of African basket weaving and textile creation. Learn traditional patterns, natural dyes, and the storytelling woven into every piece.
popularCourses.cookingDesc: Explore the diverse flavours of African cuisine. Learn to cook authentic dishes from across the continent — from Kenyan pilau to West African jollof and North African tagines.
popularCourses.woodCarvingDesc: Discover the craft of African wood carving — from masks and figurines to functional household items. Learn grain reading, tool techniques, and the spiritual meaning behind each form.

culture.description: African cultural events celebrate heritage by uniting communities through music, dance, art, food, and storytelling — honoring tradition while embracing modern African identity.

hero.description: From rare artefacts up for auction to handcrafted goods by local artisans — own a piece of the continent's living culture.

hero.description: From thundering drum festivals on the Sahel to glittering masquerade nights in Lagos - dive into the living heartbeat of the continent

byMonth.title: Find Events by Month
byMonth.subtitle: Browse African cultural events happening throughout the year
byMonth.selectPrompt: 👆 Select a month above to see events

event1.name: Bushfire Festival
event1.description: An extraordinary gathering of African artists and performers in the Kingdom of Eswatini. Music, storytelling, and community spirit in one mountain setting.

event2.name: Odwira Festival
event2.description: The Ashanti people of Ghana purify their stools and honour ancestors in this week-long ceremony filled with processions, dancing, drumming, singing and feasting.

hero.subtitle: From the ancient beat of the djembe to the global rise of Afrobeats and Amapiano — discover the sounds that shaped the world.

explore.title: Explore
explore.subtitle: Discover African sounds, artists, instruments and global influence

genre1.name: Afrobeats
genre1.description: A global phenomenon blending highlife, funk, and hip-hop. Born in Lagos, Afrobeats has become one of the world's most dominant music forces — infectious rhythms that took over dancefloors from Nairobi to New York.
genre1.artists: Burna Boy, Wizkid, Davido, Tems, Omah Lay

genre2.name: Amapiano
genre2.description: A hypnotic blend of deep house, jazz, and log drum percussion. Born in Soweto's townships, Amapiano has swept the continent and is now dominating global charts and streaming platforms.
genre2.artists: Kabza De Small, DJ Maphorisa, Tyler ICU, Focalistic

genre3.name: Benga
genre3.description: Kenya's indigenous pop style — driving acoustic guitar rhythms layered over traditional Luo melodies. Benga is the foundation of Kenyan popular music and one of East Africa's most distinctive sounds.
genre3.artists: D.O. Misiani, Ochieng Kabaselleh, Victoria Kings

genre4.name: Highlife
genre4.description: One of Africa's oldest modern genres, mixing brass band sounds with Ghanaian folk rhythms. Sophisticated, joyful, and timeless — a genre that laid the foundation for much of West African pop.
genre4.artists: E.T. Mensah, Amakye Dede, Daddy Lumba

instruments.title: Instruments of
instruments.subtitle: Ancient tools of sound that carry centuries of culture and story

kora.name: Kora
kora.description: 21-string harp-lute from West Africa. The instrument of griots — storytellers and historians.

djembe.name: Djembe
djembe.description: The world-famous goblet drum from Guinea. Central to ceremonies and community celebrations.

mbira.name: Mbira
mbira.description: Zimbabwe's thumb piano — a spiritual instrument used to communicate with ancestors.

talkingDrum.name: Talking Drum
talkingDrum.description: Hour-glass shaped drum that mimics tonal languages, used for long-distance communication.

krar.name: Krar
krar.description: A five-string lyre from Ethiopia and Eritrea with a rich resonant sound tied to ancient tradition.

balafon.name: Balafon
balafon.description: A wooden xylophone beloved across Mali, Senegal, and Côte d'Ivoire.

artists.title: Featured
artists.subtitle: Legends and rising stars from across the continent

artist1.description: Father of Afrobeat. His music was revolutionary, political, and immortal.

artist2.description: "Mama Africa" — a voice of beauty and resistance heard around the world.

artist3.description: Grammy-winning African Giant who brought Afrobeats to every continent.

artist4.description: A generation-defining voice blending soul, R&B, and Afrobeats.

artist5.description: Proved the deep connection between West African music and American blues.

artist6.description: One of Africa's most celebrated voices, fusing Wolof tradition with global sound.

artist7.description: Proved the deep connection between West African music and American blues.

influence.title: Africa's Global
influence.subtitle: How African rhythms shaped the music the whole world listens to

jazz.name: Jazz & Blues
jazz.description: The pentatonic scales, call-and-response patterns, and rhythmic complexity of African music are the direct ancestors of Jazz and Blues.

reggae.name: Reggae
reggae.description: Jamaican reggae carries deep African rhythmic heritage through the diaspora, with Rastafari explicitly rooted in Ethiopian traditions.

hiphop.name: Hip-Hop
hiphop.description: The griot tradition of spoken word, storytelling, and call-and-response is widely acknowledged as a spiritual ancestor of hip-hop.

electronic.name: Electronic Music
electronic.description: From Amapiano to Afrobeats, African rhythm structures are reshaping global club culture and mainstream pop production.

helmet.title: Technology | Magical Africa
helmet.description: Discover how Magical Africa uses proprietary AI to preserve 2,000+ African languages, digitize oral heritage, and deliver personalized cultural learning at scale.
helmet.keywords: Magical Africa technology, African AI, African language AI, African language preservation, speech recognition Africa
helmet.ogTitle: Magical Africa — AI Technology for African Heritage
helmet.ogDescription: Proprietary AI models built specifically for African linguistic complexity and cultural depth — preserving 2,000+ languages ignored by mainstream platforms.

hero.subtitle: Proprietary models built specifically for African linguistic complexity, cultural depth, and the preservation of 2,000+ languages — ignored by mainstream AI for too long.

intro.title: The Problem We're Solving
intro.description: African languages represent less than 1% of all digital content. As elders pass on, centuries of oral wisdom disappear with them. Magical Africa uses AI to reverse this — digitizing, preserving, and teaching African heritage at scale.
intro.quote: Preserving the wisdom of the elders for the children of tomorrow.

features.sectionSubtitle: Six core AI systems working together to preserve and teach African heritage.

speech.title: Speech Recognition
speech.description: Specialized AI models trained on tonal African languages and dialects — languages often ignored by mainstream platforms like Google and OpenAI. Our models understand the tonal nuances that make each language unique.

transcription.title: Oral Transcription
transcription.description: Automated digitization of oral storytelling — converting spoken heritage from elders into searchable, archivable, and shareable text. Centuries of wisdom, preserved before it is lost forever.

indexing.title: Cultural Indexing
indexing.description: Semantic AI that organizes proverbs, historical narratives, and cultural knowledge by community, region, and era — making African heritage discoverable in a way never before possible.

recommendations.title: Smart Recommendations
recommendations.description: AI-driven learning paths that match every user with the most relevant cultural stories, language lessons, and native speakers — adapting in real time to their progress and interests.

regeneration.title: Content Regeneration
regeneration.description: AI-assisted reconstruction of fragmented oral histories and automatic adaptation of traditional folklore into modern children's content — bridging generations through technology.

tools.title: Creator AI Tools
tools.description: A full suite of tools empowering elders, artists, and storytellers to publish, monetize, and preserve their heritage digitally — no technical knowledge required. The platform does the heavy lifting.

capture.title: Capture
capture.desc: Elders, artists and storytellers record oral content through the Magical Africa Creator Platform — audio, video, and text.

transcribe.title: Transcribe
transcribe.desc: Our Speech Recognition AI converts spoken African languages into text, handling tonal variations and dialects with precision.

index.title: Index
index.desc: Cultural Indexing AI categorizes and tags the content by language, region, tribe, and topic — making it fully searchable.

deliver.title: Deliver
deliver.desc: Smart Recommendation AI serves the right content to the right learner — in their language, at their pace, on any device.

cta.description: Help us build the largest digital repository of African linguistic and cultural knowledge. Whether you are a creator, learner, or investor — there is a place for you at Magical Africa.

items.subtitle: Pan-African by Design
items.title: Built for all 54 nations and the global diaspora
items.detail: Everything we build serves the full breadth of the African continent — no single culture, language, or region takes priority over another. From East to West, North to South, and across diaspora communities in Europe, the Americas, and beyond, Magical Africa is designed to represent and celebrate every corner of African heritage. Our content strategy, language support, and community features are all built with this pan-African lens at their core.

items.subtitle: Community Ownership
items.title: Creators and communities own their heritage
items.detail: The communities whose languages and stories we preserve retain full ownership and sovereignty over their content. We are custodians, not owners. Every elder, artist, and storyteller who contributes to the platform earns directly from their work through transparent revenue sharing models. We will never sell community data, license cultural knowledge without consent, or extract value from heritage without giving back to the people it belongs to.

items.subtitle: Ethical AI Stewardship
items.title: AI that serves culture, not the other way around
items.detail: Our AI models are built with community consent, cultural sensitivity, and continuous bias auditing at every stage. We reject the extractive model of data collection that has historically harmed marginalized communities. Every dataset we use is sourced ethically and with explicit permission. Communities are partners in training the models that represent their languages — and they benefit directly from the AI products built on their knowledge.

items.subtitle: Sustainable Long-Term Impact
items.title: Building for generations, not quarters
items.detail: We measure success not in monthly active users or quarterly revenue, but in languages preserved, elders recorded, and children who grow up knowing their heritage. Every product decision, partnership, and business model is evaluated through the lens of long-term cultural impact. We are playing a 50-year game — ensuring that the wisdom held by today's elders is accessible to the grandchildren of tomorrow.

items.subtitle: Digital Preservation
items.title: Saving languages before they disappear forever
items.detail: Over 600 African languages are currently classified as endangered. Without urgent digital intervention, they will vanish within a generation. Magical Africa works directly with linguistic experts, community elders, and local schools to record, transcribe, and teach these languages at scale — creating permanent digital archives that ensure no African language is lost to time, urbanization, or neglect.

items.subtitle: Radical Accessibility
items.title: Heritage should never be locked behind a paywall
items.detail: We believe every African — regardless of income, location, or technical literacy — deserves access to their own cultural heritage. That is why our core learning content is free, our platform works on low-bandwidth connections, and our mobile experience is optimized for the devices most commonly used across the continent. Premium features exist to fund the free tier, not to restrict it.
